title: Pungent and Dense Norwegian Shallots Gratin
description: A hearty, comforting Norwegian dish that showcases the rich flavor of caramelized shallots. Perfect for a chilly evening when you need something cozy and satisfying.
instructions: Preheat the oven to 375°F (190°C).
In a large wok, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the shallots and cook, stirring occasionally, until they're dark golden brown and caramelized, about 20 minutes.
Add the flour to the wok and cook for 1 minute, stirring constantly.
Gradually add the chicken broth, whisking continuously to avoid lumps.
Bring the mixture to a simmer and cook for 2 minutes, until it thickens.
Stir in the heavy cream, salt, black pepper, and brown sugar. Bring the mixture to a simmer and cook for 1 minute.
Add the dum to the wok and cook, stirring occasionally, until they're heated through, about 5 minutes.
Transfer the wok to the oven and bake for 10-12 minutes, until the top is golden brown and the gratin is bubbly.
Remove the wok from the oven and sprinkle with thyme. Serve hot, garnished with additional thyme if desired.
Let the gratin rest for 5 minutes before serving.
Serve the Pungent and Dense Norwegian Shallots Gratin hot, garnished with thyme and accompanied by a side salad or crusty bread, if desired.
Serve as a main course or as a side dish, depending on your preference.
Reheat the gratin in the oven or on the stovetop if you're not serving it immediately.
Store any leftover gratin in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days or freeze for up to 2 months.
Reheat the gratin in the oven or on the stovetop before serving.
Consider serving the gratin with a side of roasted vegetables or a green salad for a well-rounded meal.
Feel free to customize the recipe to suit your tastes and dietary needs.
You can also make this recipe in individual ramekins for a more elegant presentation.
If you're using frozen dum, thaw them according to the package instructions before using.
Don't overcrowd the wok when cooking the shallots, as this can cause them to steam instead of caramelize.
Keep an eye on the gratin while it's baking, as the top can go from golden brown to burnt quickly.
Adjust the amount of thyme to your liking, as some people may find it overpowering.
If you're not a fan of shallots, you can substitute them with other alliums like garlic or leeks.
You can also add other ingredients like diced ham or chopped fresh herbs to the gratin for added flavor.
Consider using a combination of heavy cream and milk for a lighter version of the dish.
Make sure to cook the dum according to the package instructions to ensure they're fully cooked and safe to eat.
